perturbation.original_text: In the event that such protective order or other remedy is not obtained, the receiving party agrees to furnish only that portion of the Confidential Information which it is advised by counsel is legally required to be furnished and to exercise reasonable efforts to obtain assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded such Confidential Information
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that such protective order or other remedy is not obtained, the receiving party agrees to furnish that portion of the Confidential Information which it is legally required to be furnished.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'reasonable efforts to obtain assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded such Confidential Information', the receiving party is not obligated to seek confidential treatment of the information, even if possible. This creates potential conflict with trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the requirement for the receiving party to seek assurance of confidential treatment when disclosing legally compelled information. This contradicts the spirit of the UTSA, which aims to protect trade secrets by encouraging measures to maintain confidentiality, even when disclosure is mandated.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'reasonable efforts to obtain assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded such Confidential Information' directly contradicts the UTSA's objective to safeguard trade secrets by promoting confidentiality maintenance, even when disclosure is legally necessary. While the snippets are not directly applicable, the law explanation describes the conflict as not seeking assurance of confidential treatment when disclosing legally compelled information. This contradicts the spirit of the UTSA, which aims to protect trade secrets. Removing the 'reasonable efforts' clause weakens the protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the disclosing party and its shareholders, directors, officers, employees, representatives and professional advisors from any damage, loss, cost or liability (including reasonable legal fees and the cost of enforcing this Agreement) owed to a third party arising out of the breach by the receiving party of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will indemnify the disclosing party from any damage, loss, cost or liability owed to a third party arising out of the breach by the receiving party of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit inclusion of 'reasonable legal fees and the cost of enforcing this Agreement' weakens the indemnification clause, potentially forcing the disclosing party to bear its legal costs even in cases of clear breach. This is non-compliant with laws requiring full compensation for damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting 'reasonable legal fees' from the indemnification clause, the modified text potentially contradicts the principle of expectation damages, which aims to put the injured party in as good a position as if the contract had been fully performed. Legal fees are often a necessary expense in enforcing a contract and recovering damages, and excluding them undermines the goal of full compensation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: damages | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you damages In civil cases , damages are the remedy that a party requests the court award in order to try to make the injured party whole. Typically damage awards are in the form of monetary compensation to the harmed party. Damages are imposed if the court finds that a party breached a duty under contract or violated some right. The sum of money included in the damages can be compensatory damages that are calculated based on the harmed party’s actual loses, or punitive damages intended to punish the wrongdoer. The term " actual damages " is synonymous with compensatory damages and excludes punitive damages. In a contract case , punitive damages are generally not awarded. This is because the law generally recognizes that parties should be allowed to breach a contract where it would be more economically efficient to do so. Thus, the law does not punish a party for breach, it simply seeks to put the non-breaching party back into a fair position. This can mean the court awards the non-breaching party either expectancy damages which is what the party expected to receive under the contract, reliance damages which is the economic position the party would have been in had they not relied on the contract, or restitution which is an equitable remedy to take away profits from the party that breached. There are also liquidated damages which contract parties can agree to in advance in the event of breach. In contract law, if a court determines that damages will not properly compensate the injured party, the court may choose to award specific performance . In a tort case , the injured party can receive compensatory damages to compensate for all types of losses, including direct costs for medical car, property damage, or lost wages. It can also include indirect costs such as compensating for pain and suffering or inconvenience. When a tort wrongdoer was willfully reckless or the harm was particularly bad, the court may award punitive damages in addition to compensatory damages. For certain types of injuries, statutes provide that successful parties should receive some multiple of their "actual damages" -- e.g., treble damages . Federal Material U.S. Constitution and Federal Statutes U.S. Code: 15 U.S.C. - Restraint of Trade 26 U.S.C. - Internal Revenue Code 28 U.S.C. - Recovery of Damages Against the U.S. and Foreign States 45 U.S.C., Chapter 2 - Railway Carriers Federal Court Rules Federal Rules of Civil Procedure: Rule 9(g) - Special Damages Rule 54(c) - Judgment Uniform Laws U.C.C., Article 2, Part 7 - Remedies [Last reviewed in September of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords damages tort law contract law injunctive relief monetary relief specific performance Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) tort damages THE LEGAL PROCESS courts legal practice/ethics wex articles business law contracts wex definitions civil procedure courts and procedure legal education and practice money and financial problems Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-708. Seller's Damages for Non-acceptance or Repudiation. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-708. Seller's Damages for Non-acceptance or Repudiation. (1) Subject to subsection (2) and to the provisions of this Article with respect to proof of market price (Section 2-723 ), the measure of damages for non-acceptance or repudiation by the buyer is the difference between the market price at the time and place for tender and the unpaid contract price together with any incidental damages provided in this Article (Section 2-710 ), but less expenses saved in consequence of the buyer's breach. (2) If the measure of damages provided in subsection (1) is inadequate to put the seller in as good a position as performance would have done then the measure of damages is the profit (including reasonable overhead) which the seller would have made from full performance by the buyer , together with any incidental damages provided in this Article (Section 2-710 ), due allowance for costs reasonably incurred and due credit for payments or proceeds of resale. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'reasonable legal fees and the cost of enforcing this Agreement' directly contradicts the principle of making the injured party whole. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'damages are the remedy that a party requests the court award in order to try to make the injured party whole.' By excluding legal fees, the changed text prevents the disclosing party from being fully compensated for losses incurred due to the receiving party's breach, undermining the purpose of damages as defined in the legal snippet. This creates a legal loophole where the disclosing party may not recover all costs associated with enforcing the agreement, directly contradicting the aim to restore the injured party to their original position.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party agrees and acknowledges that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach or threatened breach of the provisions of this Agreement by the receiving party or any of its shareholders, directors, officers, employees, representatives or professional advisors and that the disclosing party shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies are not the exclusive remedies for a breach by the receiving party, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity to the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party agrees and acknowledges that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach or threatened breach of the provisions of this Agreement by the receiving party or any of its shareholders, directors, officers, employees, representatives or professional advisors and that the disclosing party shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause stating that 'Such remedies are not the exclusive remedies for a breach by the receiving party, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity to the disclosing party' limits the disclosing party's potential recourse by implying that equitable relief is the only remedy. This could violate the principle of providing adequate legal remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of the phrase 'Such remedies are not the exclusive remedies for a breach by the receiving party, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity to the disclosing party' restricts the remedies available to the disclosing party. This contradicts the principle that remedies for breach should be interpreted liberally to provide adequate compensation, potentially violating the legal principle enshrined in statutes like UCC § 1-305.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. (a) The remedies provided by [the Uniform Commercial Code] must be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed but neither consequential or special damages nor penal damages may be had except as specifically provided in [the Uniform Commercial Code] or by other rule of law. (b) Any right or obligation declared by [the Uniform Commercial Code] is enforceable by action unless the provision declaring it specifies a different and limited effect. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit statement that equitable remedies are *in addition* to other available remedies, implying equitable relief is the *only* recourse. This contradicts UCC § 1-305, which states remedies should be "liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed". By limiting the potential remedies, the changed text restricts the disclosing party's ability to be made whole, thereby contradicting the UCC's directive for liberal administration of remedies.

perturbation.original_text: Each party represents and warrants that (i) such party is an entity duly organized and validly existing under the laws of its state of organization, (ii) such party has the power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform fully its obligations under this Agreement; (iii) such party is under no contractual or other legal obligation which might interfere in any way with its prompt and complete performance under this Agreement; and (iv) the person executing this Agreement on behalf of such party has been duly authorized to do so and such execution is binding upon such party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party represents and warrants that (i) such party is an entity duly organized and validly existing under the laws of its state of organization, (ii) such party has the power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform fully its obligations under this Agreement; and (iv) the person executing this Agreement on behalf of such party has been duly authorized to do so and such execution is binding upon such party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the warranty that '(iii) such party is under no contractual or other legal obligation which might interfere in any way with its prompt and complete performance under this Agreement', the contract creates risk of breach. Some jurisdictions require a warranty of no conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Warranty of No Conflict
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by jurisdiction, generally implied in commercial contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the warranty that neither party has pre-existing obligations in conflict with this NDA creates legal risk. While a specific statute is not directly violated, the absence of this warranty exposes a party to claims of breach of contract or tortious interference if a prior agreement prevents them from fully performing their obligations under the NDA. Some court decisions imply a duty not to actively conceal conflicts of interest.

perturbation.justification: The original contract includes a warranty ensuring that each party is not under any conflicting contractual or legal obligations. By removing this warranty, the modified contract increases the risk of a breach of contract if a party's pre-existing obligations interfere with their performance. The Law Explanation clarifies that this omission could lead to claims of breach of contract. Although the scraped snippets don't directly address this specific type of warranty, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight the increased legal risk and potential breach.
